data.output:
1	# Artifact Generalization & Three-Layer Run Architecture
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-29
4	Status: Approved (refined through 3 review rounds with codex + fresh-eyes)
5	
6	## Summary
7	
8	Two threads converge in this plan:
9	
10	1. **Generalize artifacts beyond markdown documents.** Diagrams, wireframes, hero images, prompts — text and binary — become first-class artifacts via media-type plurality and sidecar `.ddx.yaml` files. Generated artifacts are checked into git; regeneration is source-hash-driven.
11	2. **Three-layer run architecture.** DDx owns three explicit layered primitives: `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). The current two-class storage split (`.ddx/exec-runs/` vs `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`) collapses into one substrate.
12	
13	These threads converge: **generating an artifact is a layer-1 agent run** with `produces_artifact` metadata.
14	
15	## Decisions (resolved through review)
16	
17	- **Artifact authority:** identity present → artifact. No "managed asset" second-class concept.
18	- **FEAT-019 fate:** child of FEAT-010 (not peer). Contracts to evaluation UX; workflow shapes (comparison, replay, benchmark) move to skills library.
19	- **Server posture:** 100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP for all DDx state, sequenced by impact. Writes case-by-case as workflows demand. Only `artifactRegenerate` write surface added in this plan.
20	- **Generated artifacts:** committed to git unconditionally. Size guard (50MB warn / 100MB hard error) with actionable git-lfs guidance; no auto-init / auto-`.gitattributes`.
21	- **CONTRACT-003:** amendments allowed if FEAT-006 work surfaces gaps. The mountable Cobra root export (below) is a load-bearing audit item.
22	- **Run substrate:** single on-disk shape; layer-2 and layer-3 add metadata. `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` collapse.
23	- **No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.** Comparison, replay, benchmark, etc. are skill compositions, never enshrined in Go core or specs.
24	- **Loop ownership sharpened:** DDx owns mechanical queue drain (`ddx work`); content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue reconciliation beads") remain plugin/HELIX territory.
25	- **CLI verbs:** `ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` promoted to top-level. Hard-deprecate `ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx agent execute-loop` (exit non-zero with bare redirect message — no aliases, no grace period).
26	- **Generate-artifact layer placement:** pure-return generators (agent returns bytes/text, DDx writes file) are layer-1. Repo-editing generators are layer-2 with bead-less worktree.
27	- **Quorum dispatch:** moves to skill composition. `--quorum` flag goes away; replaced by `compare-prompts` skill.
28	- **`ddx agent` passthrough is structural:** DDx imports the upstream agent module's Cobra root and mounts it under `ddx agent`. Notionally `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))`. DDx does not enumerate per-subcommand wrappers; the entire upstream tree is the source of truth.
29	
30	## Three-layer architecture
31	
32	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
33	|---|---|---|---|---|
34	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + agent config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream `ddx-agent` per CONTRACT-003 |
35	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + agent-run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
36	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
37	
38	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
39	
40	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 if the generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata.
41	
42	## Frame artifact updates
43	
44	### docs/helix/00-discover/product-vision.md
45	- Thesis: "documents AI agents consume" → "artifacts agents produce and consume."
46	- Artifact-management bullet: non-document media + generators.
47	- One sentence on producer/consumer space (no separate matrix).
48	- New Design-Philosophy subsection: **Three-layer run architecture** — `run` / `try` / `work` named as DDx's loop ownership.
49	- KVPs: "Multi-media artifact graph"; "Three-layer run architecture (run/try/work)"; note invocation is upstream.
50	- Principle #1: "Artifacts are the product (documents primary)."
51	
52	### docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md
53	- Summary: artifacts (multi-media); three-layer run architecture; one substrate.
54	- Problem: add "no provenance for generated artifacts."
55	- Goals: reword #1; add three-layer-architecture goal; add generated-artifact-regeneration goal; add "100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP."
56	- Non-goals: sharpen loop wording (mechanical drain in scope; supervisory content-aware decisions out). Add: "DDx does not catalog run types beyond the three layers."
57	- FEAT-001/005/006/007/008/010/019/021 blurbs updated.
58	
59	### FEAT-001-cli.md
60	- Promote `run` / `try` / `work` to top-level.
61	- `ddx agent` mounts upstream Cobra root structurally; no DDx-defined leaf subcommands beneath it.
62	- Hard-deprecation handlers for `ddx agent {run, execute-bead, execute-loop}`.
63	- New namespaces: `ddx runs` (cross-layer evidence introspection), `ddx tries` (layer-2 specifically), `ddx work workers` (layer-3 worker management).
64	- Remove FEAT-001:92 backward-compatibility clause for `ddx agent execute-loop`.
65	
66	### FEAT-005-artifacts.md
67	- Identity broadens to non-markdown via sidecar `.ddx.yaml`.
68	- Add `media_type` and `generated_by` fields.
69	- Authority rule documented: identity present → artifact.
70	- Terminology cleanup ("identity," not "ddx: identity").
71	
72	### FEAT-006-agent-service.md
73	- Tighten to layer-1 consumer-side wrapper that powers `ddx run`.
74	- `ddx agent` is structural passthrough (mount upstream Cobra root).
75	- Non-bead Profile/Permissions selection (artifact-keyed path).
76	- Migration table: what stays in FEAT-006 (CONTRACT-003 boundary, profile/permissions), what moves to FEAT-010 (worktree, merge/preserve, evidence bundles, queue drain), what moves to FEAT-001 (CLI surface).
77	- Session-log boundary clarified: DDx owns the envelope/pointer in evidence bundles; upstream owns inner log shape.
78	
79	### FEAT-010-executions.md (substantial refactor)
80	- Three-layer architecture explicit and load-bearing.
81	- Substrate unification: one record shape; layer metadata.
82	- Migrate `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` to one layout.
83	- Specify `ddx work` no-progress / stop conditions.
84	- Narrow read-only amendment for `artifactRegenerate` only.
85	- No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.
86	
87	### FEAT-007-doc-graph.md
88	- Sidecar scanner refactor; `media_type`; `generated_by` edge with separate staleness rule (provenance, not authority — does not cascade like `depends_on`); version bump on Complete-status feature.
89	- 100% read endpoints for graph state, generated-artifact metadata, sidecar content.
90	
91	### FEAT-008-web-ui.md
92	- Media-type-aware rendering (markdown, mermaid SVG, excalidraw embed, image inline, PDF embed).
93	- Regenerate action wired to `artifactRegenerate` mutation; reconcile with existing FEAT-008:391 weak Re-run requirement.
94	- Layer-aware run views (`work` → `try` → `run` drill-down).
95	
96	### FEAT-019-agent-evaluation.md
97	- Contract to evaluation UX (comparison views, grading rubric storage and display, benchmark result aggregation).
98	- Workflow shapes → skills library.
99	- Remove peer-to-FEAT-010 assertions (FEAT-019:40, :404).
100	
101	### FEAT-021-dashboard-ui.md
102	- Layer-aware run-history routes against unified substrate.
103	
104	### FEAT-014, FEAT-013
105	- One-sentence touches: cost attribution and concurrency for generate-artifact.
106	
107	## `ddx agent` subcommand fates
108	
109	`ddx agent` becomes a structural mount of the upstream agent CLI Cobra root. Per-file fate in DDx:
110	
111	**Delete with prejudice (upstream owns these via mounted subtree):**
112	- `agent_list.go`
113	- `agent_capabilities.go`
114	- `agent_doctor.go`
115	- `agent_check.go`
116	- `agent_models.go`
117	- `agent_providers.go`
118	- `agent_route_status.go`
119	- `agent_catalog.go`
120	- `agent_reindex.go`
121	- `agent_benchmark.go` (replaced by `benchmark-suite` skill)
122	- `agent_replay.go` (replaced by `replay-bead` skill)
123	
124	**Move to other DDx namespaces (DDx-only concerns):**
125	- `agent_executions.go` → `tries_*.go`
126	- `agent_log.go` → `runs_log.go`
127	- `agent_metrics.go` → `runs_metrics.go`
128	- `agent_usage.go` → folded into `runs_metrics.go`
129	- `agent_workers.go` → `work_workers.go`
130	
131	**DDx-side overrides on the mounted subtree:**
132	- Deprecation handlers for `agent run` / `agent execute-bead` / `agent execute-loop`.
133	- `agent condense` (DDx-specific output filter — keep as override or move upstream during impl pass).
134	
135	**`agent_cmd.go` itself thins to:** mount upstream Cobra root + register DDx overrides.
136	
137	## Skill library (DDx-shipped workflows)
138	
139	Per FEAT-011's path model: source at `skills/ddx/`, embedded at `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`, init-time copies to `.claude/skills/ddx/`, `.agents/skills/ddx/`, `.ddx/skills/ddx/`.
140	
141	- `compare-prompts` — N-arm dispatch + aggregation (replaces `--quorum` flag and `agent benchmark`)
142	- `replay-bead` — re-run with altered conditions, baseline diff (replaces `agent replay`)
143	- `benchmark-suite` — compare across prompt matrix
144	- `adversarial-review`
145	- `effort-estimate`
146	- `bead-breakdown`
147	
148	## Bead breakdown
149	
150	**Frame updates (sequential where dependencies require):**
151	
152	1. Update product-vision.md (+ three-layer subsection)
153	2. Update prd.md (+ three-layer goal, sharpened loop non-goal, no-catalog non-goal)
154	3. Update FEAT-001 (CLI surface refactor; remove backcompat clause; new namespaces) — **hard predecessor for #6, #7**
155	4. Update FEAT-005 (multi-media identity refactor)
156	5. CONTRACT-003 audit — confirm `ddx-agent` exports a mountable Cobra root and other gaps
157	6. *(Conditional)* Open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent` if #5 surfaces gaps
158	7. Update FEAT-006 (layer-1 consumer; structural passthrough; non-bead Profile selection; migration table; session-log envelope boundary)
159	8. Refactor FEAT-010 (three-layer architecture; substrate unification; on-disk migration; no-progress stop conditions; narrow `artifactRegenerate` amendment)
160	9. Update FEAT-007 (sidecar refactor; media_type; generated_by; separate staleness; version bump)
161	10. Contract FEAT-019 to evaluation UX
162	11. Update FEAT-008 (media rendering, regenerate action, layer-aware run views)
163	12. Update FEAT-021 (layer-aware run-history routes)
164	13. Update FEAT-014 (cost attribution for generate-artifact)
165	14. Update FEAT-013 (concurrency note for regenerate)
166	15. **Repo-wide audit & verb migration** — atomic with #3:
167	    - Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands (list above).
168	    - Migrate DDx-only run/evidence subcommands to `runs`/`tries`/`work workers`.
169	    - Register DDx overrides on the mounted subtree (deprecations + condense).
170	    - Delete `agent_reindex.go` outright.
171	    - Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to new verbs.
172	
173	**Design artifacts (concurrent with frame):**
174	
175	16. Architecture: artifact identity schema (concurrent with #4)
176	17. Architecture: graph schema with media_type + generated_by + separate staleness (concurrent with #9)
177	18. Architecture: three-layer run substrate — record shape, layer metadata, on-disk layout, migration, `ddx work` stop conditions (concurrent with #8)
178	19. Sidecar `.ddx.yaml` schema design
179	
180	**Read-coverage thread:**
181	
182	20. Read-coverage audit — enumerate every CLI-visible read; map to HTTP/MCP coverage; identify gaps
183	21. Per-gap beads — sequenced by user-visibility impact, land case-by-case (this is a pointer to ongoing work, not a single bead)
184	
185	**Skill library:**
186	
187	22. `compare-prompts` skill
188	23. `replay-bead` skill
189	24. `benchmark-suite` skill
190	25. `adversarial-review` skill
191	26. `effort-estimate` skill
192	27. `bead-breakdown` skill
193	
194	**Implementation (P2, after frame & design):**
195	
196	- CLI: `ddx run` / `ddx try` top-level commands; mount upstream agent Cobra root; deprecation handlers
197	- CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work workers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)
198	- CLI: `ddx artifact regenerate <id>`, `ddx artifact list --media-type <t>`
199	- Storage: sidecar reader/writer; size-guard threshold/warning/tests
200	- Graph: `generated_by` edges + provenance staleness rule
201	- Run framework: refactor to one substrate per #18; generator metadata; `ddx work` no-progress detection
202	- Server: `artifactRegenerate` write endpoint
203	- Server UI: media-type renderers; regenerate action; layer-aware run history
204	- Migration: passive `media_type` only (read-time inference from extension); on-disk run-record migration per #18
205	
206	## Constraints
207	
208	- Beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — old verbs are referenced in scripts/library/personas/init help text/CI; an isolated #3 break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced this plan.
209	- Bead #5 (CONTRACT-003 audit) gates #7 (FEAT-006). #6 conditional.
210	- Architecture sketches (#16–#19) run concurrent with their corresponding frame beads, not after — keeps frame/design from drifting.
211	- Read-coverage epic (#20–#21) is independent and not gated on this plan.
212	
213	## Refinement provenance
214	
215	Refined through 3 rounds of adversarial review (codex via `ddx agent run --harness codex` + fresh-eyes general-purpose subagent in parallel) plus user-resolved questions Q1–Q12. Key user decisions: artifact authority (Q1), FEAT-019 child-of-010 (Q2), reads-matter-writes-case-by-case (Q3), check-in unconditionally (Q4), CONTRACT-003 amendments allowed (Q5), substrate unification (Q6), no run-type catalog (Q7), no v1-gate framing (Q8), FEAT-019 contracts (Q9), generate-artifact split layer-1/layer-2 (Q10), quorum-as-skill (Q11), structural passthrough not per-command (Q12 follow-up).
216

adversarial-review

SKILL.md

1	---
2	name: ddx
3	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
4	---
5	
6	# DDx
7	
8	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
9	document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
10	items with acceptance criteria), an agent service (harness dispatch
11	with profile-first routing), a persona system (bindable AI
12	personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
13	personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
14	skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
15	CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.
16	
17	## How this skill works
18	
19	The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
20	router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.
21	
22	**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
23	matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
24	not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
25	guidance, not this overview alone.**
26	
27	## Vocabulary
28	
29	Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
30	exact definitions.
31	
32	- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
33	  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
34	- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
35	  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
36	- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
37	  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
38	- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
39	  `ddx bead blocked`.
40	- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
41	  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
42	- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
43	  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
44	  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
45	- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` (alias for
46	  `ddx agent execute-loop`); see `reference/work.md`.
47	- **Execute-bead** — the primitive: run one agent on one bead in an
48	  isolated worktree. `ddx agent execute-bead <id>`. The loop wraps
49	  this.
50	- **Execute-loop** — the queue-drain orchestrator; picks ready beads
51	  one at a time, calls execute-bead, closes on success, unclaims on
52	  failure. `ddx agent execute-loop` (or `ddx work`).
53	- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010); broader
54	  than execute-bead. Includes execution definitions, execution
55	  records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/executions/<id>/`.
56	- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
57	  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
58	  below).
59	- **Harness** — the abstraction DDx uses to dispatch an agent
60	  (subprocess for claude/codex/gemini; embedded for the built-in
61	  `agent` harness). `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent capabilities`.
62	- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
63	  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
64	  addendum to `ddx agent run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
65	- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
66	  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
67	  personas.
68	- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
69	  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
70	- **Profile** — intent-based routing policy for `ddx agent run` and
71	  `ddx work`: `default` (local-first with cloud escalation), `cheap`
72	  (local-only), `fast` (cloud-fast), `smart` (high-quality cloud).
73	  `--profile default|cheap|fast|smart`.
74	- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
75	  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
76	  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
77	  `ddx install <name>`.
78	- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
79	  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
80	  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
81	- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
82	  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
83	  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx's `ddx agent run`
84	  (especially with `--quorum`) is the portable primitive; how a
85	  harness wraps that in a subagent is harness business. This skill
86	  does not take a position on subagent orchestration.
87	- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
88	  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
89	- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
90	  `ddx upgrade`.
91	- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
92	  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
93	  acceptance criteria. **Quorum review** (`ddx agent run
94	  --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>`) dispatches a review prompt
95	  across multiple harnesses and aggregates. See `reference/review.md`.
96	- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
97	  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
98	  via `spec-id`.
99	- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
100	  at its governing artifact.
101	
102	## Intent router
103	
104	Before responding, read the matching file.
105	
106	| User says / asks about | Read this file |
107	|---|---|
108	| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
109	| "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "run work", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
110	| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
111	| "run an agent", "dispatch", harnesses, profiles, models, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
112	| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |
113	
114	If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
115	run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
116	user which concept they mean rather than guessing.
117	
118	## Top-level policy reminders
119	
120	These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
121	reference file; do not violate them.
122	
123	- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
124	  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
125	  bead history and cannot be audited.
126	- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
127	  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
128	  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
129	  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
130	- **Preserve execute-bead commit history.** Branches containing
131	  `ddx agent execute-bead` commits carry an audit trail. **Never
132	  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
133	  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
134	  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
135	  branches.
136	- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
137	  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
138	  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
139	  manual parallel work should too.
140	- **Profile-first agent dispatch.** Default to
141	  `ddx agent run --profile smart` (or `cheap`/`fast` for specific
142	  intents). Only override with `--harness`/`--model`/`--effort`
143	  when pinning for a controlled test or known provider bug.
144	
145	## Links out
146	
147	- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
148	- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
149	  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
150	  executions, and skills features.
151	- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
152	  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
153	- Open standard this skill conforms to:
154	  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).
155

1	---
2	name: adversarial-review
3	description: Run an adversarial review of a plan, spec, bead, or PR by dispatching to multiple harnesses with adversarial framing and aggregating findings. Use when you want pressure-testing against a single-model blindspot, high-stakes review, or multi-round critique before committing to a direction.
4	---
5	
6	# Adversarial Review
7	
8	A workflow skill for pressure-testing a target (plan, spec, bead, code change,
9	or PR) by dispatching adversarial review prompts to multiple harnesses and
10	aggregating the findings. Replaces ad-hoc `--quorum` invocations with a
11	structured, repeatable workflow.
12	
13	## When to use
14	
15	- **Plans and specs** before breaking into beads — catch ambiguity and missing
16	  constraints early.
17	- **High-stakes beads** — migrations, auth, API contracts, security surface.
18	- **Controversial changes** — one model's opinion is not enough; different model
19	  families catch different failure modes.
20	- **Multi-round refinement** — run 2–3 rounds; use findings to revise; re-run
21	  until BLOCKING count drops to zero.
22	
23	Single-harness bead review (`ddx bead review <id>`) is fine for routine work.
24	Reserve adversarial-review for the cases that justify the cost.
25	
26	## Workflow
27	
28	### 1. Prepare the target
29	
30	Gather the content to review into a single prompt file. Include:
31	
32	- The artifact being reviewed (plan text, spec section, bead description + AC,
33	  or diff / PR summary).
34	- The question you want pressure-tested ("Is this design complete?", "What are
35	  the failure modes?", "What does this spec leave undefined?").
36	- Any governing artifacts the reviewer should check against (FEAT-\* IDs,
37	  CONTRACT-\* IDs, ADR links).
38	
39	Write the prompt to a file, e.g. `review-target.md`:
40	
41	```markdown
42	## Target
43	
44	<paste plan, spec excerpt, or diff>
45	
46	## Governing artifacts
47	
48	- FEAT-010: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-executions.md
49	- (add others)
50	
51	## Review question
52	
53	Find BLOCKING issues: ambiguities, missing constraints, design gaps,
54	internal contradictions, or anything that would cause implementation
55	rework. Be adversarial — your job is to find problems, not validate.
56	```
57	
58	### 2. Dispatch to multiple harnesses
59	
60	Run each harness in parallel using `ddx agent run --harness <name>`.
61	Use `--profile smart` (or `--harness` for explicit control). Route to
62	harnesses from different model families for maximum coverage.
63	
64	```bash
65	# Two-harness adversarial dispatch (parallel)
66	ddx agent run --harness codex --profile smart \
67	  --prompt review-target.md \
68	  > findings-codex.md &
69	
70	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart \
71	  --prompt review-target.md \
72	  > findings-claude.md &
73	
74	wait
75	```
76	
77	For three harnesses:
78	
79	```bash
80	ddx agent run --harness codex  --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md  &
81	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-claude.md &
82	ddx agent run --harness gemini --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-gemini.md &
83	wait
84	```
85	
86	### 3. Require structured output
87	
88	Each harness must produce findings in this format. Include this contract
89	in the prompt:
90	
91	```markdown
92	## Output contract
93	
94	Produce findings as:
95	
96	### Findings
97	
98	| Severity | Area | Finding |
99	|---|---|---|
100	| BLOCKING | <area> | <specific issue> |
101	| WARNING  | <area> | <specific issue> |
102	| NOTE     | <area> | <observation> |
103	
104	### Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK
105	
106	### Summary
107	<2–4 sentences>
108	```
109	
110	A finding with no evidence is invalid. Cite the specific line, section,
111	or gap that caused the finding. "Looks fine" is not a finding.
112	
113	### 4. Aggregate
114	
115	Read all findings files and collate:
116	
117	1. **BLOCKING** items from any harness → escalate immediately. One BLOCKING
118	   is enough to stop.
119	2. **WARNING** items with ≥2 harnesses in agreement → treat as BLOCKING.
120	3. **WARNING** items unique to one harness → review manually, weigh cost.
121	4. **NOTE** items → log; address in a follow-up pass or ignore.
122	
123	Surface disagreements explicitly. If harness A says BLOCKING and harness B
124	says NOTE for the same area, show both verdicts — do not silently collapse
125	to the weaker one.
126	
127	```bash
128	# Combine findings for review
129	cat findings-codex.md findings-claude.md > findings-combined.md
130	```
131	
132	### 5. Iterate until clean
133	
134	Revise the target based on BLOCKING findings. Re-run the adversarial review
135	against the revised version. Typical well-scoped plans converge in 2–3
136	rounds. Stop when:
137	
138	- No harness returns a BLOCKING verdict, AND
139	- All WARNINGs have been explicitly accepted or addressed.
140	
141	Record the round count and final verdict in the bead evidence:
142	
143	```bash
144	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body findings-combined.md
145	```
146	
147	## Adversarial framing
148	
149	The adversarial framing instruction to include in every review prompt:
150	
151	> You are a critic, not a validator. Your job is to find every way this
152	> could fail, every constraint it leaves undefined, every assumption it
153	> bakes in without stating, every interface it leaves ambiguous. A BLOCKING
154	> finding is anything that would cause implementation rework, a migration
155	> hazard, or a spec gap that agents will interpret differently. Do not
156	> balance criticism with praise — a useful adversarial review is entirely
157	> about what is wrong.
158	
159	## Personas
160	
161	Bind a `specification-enforcer` persona for the strictest checks:
162	
163	```bash
164	ddx agent run --harness codex \
165	  --persona specification-enforcer \
166	  --prompt review-target.md
167	```
168	
169	The `specification-enforcer` persona refuses drift from governing artifacts
170	and surfaces spec gaps others miss.
171	
172	## Anti-patterns
173	
174	- **Single-harness adversarial review.** The point is cross-model coverage.
175	  One harness is bead review, not adversarial review.
176	- **No structured output contract.** Free-form critique is unaggregateable.
177	  Always include the findings table + verdict contract in the prompt.
178	- **Collapsing disagreements.** If harnesses disagree, show both verdicts.
179	  Hiding disagreement defeats the purpose.
180	- **Running adversarial review on routine beads.** Reserve for high-stakes
181	  or controversial work. Routine beads use `ddx bead review`.
182	- **Stopping after one round.** One round finds problems. Iteration verifies
183	  that the revision actually resolves them.
184	
185	## CLI reference
186	
187	```bash
188	# Parallel adversarial dispatch
189	ddx agent run --harness codex  --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md  &
190	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt review-target.md > findings-claude.md &
191	wait
192	
193	# With persona
194	ddx agent run --harness codex --persona specification-enforcer \
195	  --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md
196	
197	# Store findings as evidence
198	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body findings-combined.md
199	
200	# Bead review (routine, single-harness — not this skill)
201	ddx bead review <id>
202	ddx bead review <id> --execute --harness claude
203	```
204

1	---
2	name: bead-breakdown
3	description: Break a spec, plan, epic, or free-form task into execution-ready beads by filing `ddx bead create` calls with inline context, acceptance criteria, and dependency wiring. Use when a governing artifact, approved plan, or epic needs to be translated into a queue of actionable beads.
4	---
5	
6	# Bead Breakdown
7	
8	A workflow skill for decomposing a spec, plan, epic, or free-form task into
9	execution-ready beads. Produces a set of `ddx bead create` calls with inline
10	context, acceptance criteria, and dependency edges — so the resulting beads can
11	be picked up cold by `ddx work` with no additional human narration.
12	
13	## When to use
14	
15	- **After plan approval** — translate an approved plan into a bead queue before
16	  implementation begins.
17	- **Epic decomposition** — break a large feature (FEAT-\*, milestone, user story)
18	  into child beads when the work exceeds one session or touches multiple
19	  subsystems.
20	- **Backlog creation** — file a batch of beads from a spec or roadmap document.
21	- **Effort-estimate follow-up** — when `effort-estimate` returns
22	  `NEEDS_BREAKDOWN`, use this skill to produce the child beads.
23	
24	Single-session, single-subsystem work does not need breakdown — file one bead
25	directly. Reserve this skill for work that genuinely spans multiple files,
26	agents, or sessions.
27	
28	## Workflow
29	
30	### 1. Read the governing artifact
31	
32	Before filing any beads, read the source material in full:
33	
34	- The plan, spec, or epic (FEAT-\*, plan-\*.md, or free-form description).
35	- Any CONTRACT-\*, ADR-\*, or FEAT-\* artifacts it references.
36	- Existing beads that may already cover part of the scope
37	  (`ddx bead list --label area:<subsystem>`).
38	
39	If a `spec-id` exists (FEAT-\* or similar), every child bead should carry it.
40	
41	### 2. Identify the decomposition units
42	
43	Each bead should:
44	
45	- Touch at most ~3 files (strongly prefer fewer).
46	- Stay within one subsystem (CLI, storage, server, frontend, library, etc.).
47	- Have a single, testable outcome.
48	- Be completable in one agent session.
49	
50	If the source spans multiple subsystems, create an **epic bead** first, then
51	file child beads that depend on it. The epic provides a stable `spec-id` anchor
52	for its children.
53	
54	```
55	Epic: "Wire multi-harness quorum into ddx bead review"
56	├── Task: Add --quorum flag to ddx bead review command
57	├── Task: Dispatch the review prompt to N harnesses concurrently
58	├── Task: Aggregate per-harness verdicts into a structured report
59	└── Task: Add integration test using script harness for 2-arm quorum
60	```
61	
62	Name each unit with an imperative title that states the outcome, not the
63	activity. "Add `--quorum` flag to `ddx bead review`" beats "Quorum work".
64	
65	### 3. Draft each bead
66	
67	For each unit, draft:
68	
69	**Title** — imperative, specific, names the outcome.
70	
71	**Description** — must stand alone. An isolated agent executing the bead has
72	no prior context. Include:
73	
74	- What to change and why (inline the relevant facts; no links to ephemeral
75	  sources like chat or PR discussions).
76	- Root cause or motivation if it's a bug.
77	- File scope: explicit in-scope files and an out-of-scope list to prevent drift.
78	- Any implementation hint that narrows the search space (function name, line
79	  number, data structure, protocol detail).
80	
81	**Acceptance criteria** — every criterion is a command that passes:
82	
83	```
84	1. `cd cli && go test ./internal/bead/... -run TestQuorum` passes.
85	2. `ddx bead review <id> --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex` exits 0
86	   and prints a structured findings table.
87	3. `ddx bead review <id> --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude` exits non-zero
88	   with "quorum requires at least 2 harnesses".
89	```
90	
91	"Works correctly" is not an AC. "Looks good" is not an AC. Each line must be
92	mechanically verifiable.
93	
94	**Labels** — `area:<subsystem>` + `kind:<category>` at minimum. Add
95	`plan-<date>` when beads implement a specific plan.
96	
97	**`spec-id`** — the governing FEAT-\* or plan identifier, when one exists.
98	
99	**Type** — `task` for implementation, `bug` for defects, `epic` for
100	multi-bead containers, `chore` for non-functional work.
101	
102	### 4. Wire dependencies
103	
104	After filing all beads, wire the dependency DAG. A bead B that depends on A
105	must not enter the ready queue until A closes.
106	
107	```bash
108	ddx bead dep add <B-id> <A-id>   # B depends on A
109	```
110	
111	Common dependency patterns:
112	
113	- **Sequential:** each bead depends on the previous (strict ordering).
114	- **Fan-out from epic:** all child tasks depend on the epic bead (or on each
115	  other where implementation order matters).
116	- **Shared precondition:** multiple tasks depend on a single setup task (schema
117	  migration, API contract, shared fixture).
118	
119	Do not wire dependencies that do not exist. An unnecessary dep blocks work
120	without benefit.
121	
122	### 5. Verify the queue shape
123	
124	After filing, check the queue reflects the expected shape:
125	
126	```bash
127	ddx bead list --label plan-<date>   # all beads for this plan
128	ddx bead ready                      # beads with satisfied deps
129	ddx bead blocked                    # beads waiting on deps
130	ddx bead dep tree <epic-id>         # full dependency tree
131	```
132	
133	Confirm that the beads with no predecessors appear in `ddx bead ready` and that
134	downstream beads appear in `ddx bead blocked`.
135	
136	### 6. Commit the tracker state
137	
138	Bead creation writes to `.ddx/beads.jsonl`. Commit after all beads and deps are
139	filed:
140	
141	```bash
142	git add .ddx/beads.jsonl
143	git commit -m "chore: file beads for <plan or epic title>"
144	```
145	
146	## Sizing heuristics
147	
148	| Signal | Action |
149	|---|---|
150	| Bead touches > 3 files | Split into smaller beads |
151	| Bead spans 2+ subsystems | Split at the subsystem boundary |
152	| AC requires > 3 verifiable commands | Consider splitting |
153	| Estimated time > 4 hours (from `effort-estimate`) | Decompose before filing |
154	| Description needs > ~200 words to be self-contained | Usually too large |
155	| Bead has no testable AC | Under-specified; resolve before filing |
156	
157	## Dispatch-based breakdown
158	
159	For large specs where the breakdown itself is complex, dispatch the breakdown
160	to an agent:
161	
162	```bash
163	# Write the breakdown prompt
164	cat > breakdown-target.md << 'EOF'
165	## Source
166	
167	<paste plan excerpt or FEAT-* section>
168	
169	## Governing artifacts
170	
171	- FEAT-010: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-executions.md
172	
173	## Breakdown question
174	
175	Decompose the above into execution-ready beads. For each bead, produce:
176	- Imperative title
177	- Description (inline context, file scope, out-of-scope list)
178	- Acceptance criteria (commands that pass)
179	- Labels (area:*, kind:*)
180	- Dependencies on other beads in the set
181	
182	Output the breakdown as a numbered list. Then output the `ddx bead create`
183	commands I should run to file them, in dependency order.
184	EOF
185	
186	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart \
187	  --prompt breakdown-target.md \
188	  > breakdown.md
189	```
190	
191	Review the agent output before running the `ddx bead create` commands. The
192	agent produces a draft; you own the final bead descriptions.
193	
194	## Anti-patterns
195	
196	- **Vague titles.** "Auth work" or "Fix stuff" — an agent executing cold cannot
197	  orient. Use the outcome as the title.
198	- **Description is a link.** "See the plan at docs/..." — the agent in an
199	  isolated worktree can read that file, but the bead must still be self-contained
200	  for the case where the file moves or is renamed. Inline the facts.
201	- **AC is a sentence.** "The feature should work." Gives the agent wiggle room
202	  to claim success without evidence. Every AC line is a command that exits 0 or
203	  a named assertion.
204	- **One bead per epic.** An epic bead is a container, not an implementation
205	  unit. Always file child task beads beneath it.
206	- **Missing out-of-scope list.** Without explicit scope negation, agents drift
207	  into adjacent cleanup. List what the bead does NOT touch.
208	- **Implicit dependencies.** "After the auth PR lands" — wire it with
209	  `ddx bead dep add`. The queue cannot enforce implicit ordering.
210	- **No tests in AC.** Implementation without a test command in AC means the
211	  bead can close without verification. Every implementation bead needs at least
212	  one test command.
213	
214	## CLI reference
215	
216	```bash
217	# Create a bead
218	ddx bead create "Title" --type task \
219	  --labels area:cli,kind:task,plan-2026-04-29 \
220	  --description "..." \
221	  --acceptance "1. go test ./... passes\n2. ddx bead list shows ..." \
222	  --set spec-id=FEAT-010
223	
224	# Create an epic
225	ddx bead create "Epic: <title>" --type epic \
226	  --labels area:<subsystem>,kind:epic \
227	  --description "..."
228	
229	# Wire dependencies (B depends on A)
230	ddx bead dep add <B-id> <A-id>
231	
232	# Inspect the queue after filing
233	ddx bead list --label plan-2026-04-29
234	ddx bead ready
235	ddx bead blocked
236	ddx bead dep tree <epic-id>
237	
238	# Commit the tracker state
239	git add .ddx/beads.jsonl
240	git commit -m "chore: file beads for <plan title>"
241	
242	# Dispatch breakdown to an agent
243	ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart \
244	  --prompt breakdown-target.md > breakdown.md
245	```
246

agent_run_default_route_test.go

agent_run_profile_test.go

input.content:
---
name: compare-prompts
description: Dispatch the same prompt across N agent invocations (different harnesses, models, personas, or prompt variants) and aggregate the results into a single comparison report. Use when you need to benchmark prompt variants, run a quorum-style consensus check, compare model outputs side-by-side, or replay a prompt under altered conditions. Replaces the deprecated `--quorum` flag and `ddx agent benchmark` CLI.
---

# Compare Prompts

A workflow skill for N-arm dispatch and result aggregation. Composes
`ddx agent run` (the layer-1 invocation primitive) into a comparison
matrix: same prompt, varied conditions, structured aggregation.

This skill replaces two retired surfaces:

- `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy>` — the inline quorum flag.
- `ddx agent benchmark` — the per-prompt benchmark CLI.

Both collapse into the same workflow shape: dispatch N invocations,
aggregate their outputs, render a verdict.

## When to use

- **Prompt benchmarking** — compare two prompt variants on the same
  task to decide which one ships.
- **Model comparison** — same prompt across `claude` / `codex` /
  `gemini` (or different models within one harness) to see where
  outputs diverge.
- **Consensus checks (quorum)** — run a review prompt across N
  harnesses and require majority/unanimous agreement before acting.
- **Persona comparison** — same prompt under different personas to
  see how role framing changes the answer.
- **Replay under altered conditions** — re-run a prior prompt with a
  changed model, profile, or context to A/B a hypothesis.

For pressure-testing a plan or spec with adversarial framing, prefer
the `adversarial-review` skill — it layers a critic-only prompt
contract on top of this same N-arm shape.

## Workflow

### 1. Define the comparison axis

Pick exactly one axis to vary. Mixing axes makes results
uninterpretable.

| Axis | Vary | Hold constant |
|---|---|---|
| harness | `--harness claude`, `--harness codex`, `--harness gemini` | prompt, profile, persona |
| model | `--model <a>`, `--model <b>` | harness, prompt, persona |
| prompt | prompt-v1.md, prompt-v2.md | harness, model, persona |
| persona | `--persona reviewer`, `--persona architect` | harness, prompt, model |
| profile | `--profile cheap`, `--profile smart` | harness, prompt, persona |

If you must vary two axes (e.g., harness × model), enumerate the full
matrix explicitly and label each arm.

### 2. Author the prompt

Write a single prompt file. Include an **output contract** so each
arm produces structurally comparable output:

```markdown
## Task

<the actual ask — code review, plan critique, design question, etc.>

## Output contract

Produce your answer as:

### Verdict: <one line>

### Reasoning
<2–6 sentences>

### Specific findings
- <finding 1>
- <finding 2>
```

Without an output contract, aggregation degenerates into freeform
prose comparison. The contract is what makes N-arm dispatch
mechanically aggregateable.

### 3. Dispatch the arms in parallel

Run each arm as a backgrounded `ddx agent run`, capturing stdout
to a per-arm file. Wait for all arms before aggregating.

```bash
PROMPT=prompt.md
OUT=compare-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
mkdir -p "$OUT"

# Three-arm harness comparison
ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/claude.md" 2> "$OUT/claude.err" &
ddx agent run --harness codex  --profile smart --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/codex.md"  2> "$OUT/codex.err"  &
ddx agent run --harness gemini --profile smart --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/gemini.md" 2> "$OUT/gemini.err" &
wait
```

Per-arm conventions:

- One file per arm, named for the varied axis value (`claude.md`,
  `codex.md`, …) — not `arm1.md` / `arm2.md`.
- Capture stderr separately. Failures in one arm should not silently
  drop a result row from the aggregate.
- Tag arms with the full invocation in a header comment when the
  filename alone doesn't disambiguate (e.g., model+persona variants).

### 4. Verify each arm produced output

Before aggregating, confirm each arm exited cleanly and produced
output that satisfies the prompt's output contract. An arm that
errored or returned empty must be marked as such — never silently
omitted.

```bash
for arm in "$OUT"/*.md; do
  if [ ! -s "$arm" ]; then
    echo "ARM FAILED: $arm"
  fi
done
```

### 5. Aggregate

Aggregate per the comparison axis. The aggregation policy depends on
the use case:

**Quorum (consensus check):**

- `unanimous` — every arm must produce the same verdict.
- `majority` — strictly more than half the arms agree.
- `any` — at least one arm produced the verdict (lowest bar).

Surface disagreement explicitly. If 2 of 3 arms say APPROVE and 1
says BLOCK, the report shows all three verdicts plus the policy
outcome. Hidden disagreement defeats the purpose of multi-arm
dispatch.

**Benchmark (prompt or model variant comparison):**

- Tabulate the structured output across arms (verdict + findings).
- Flag arms that diverged from the others on the verdict line.
- Note quality differentials — but do not pick a winner mechanically;
  human judgment closes the comparison.

**Replay (altered-conditions re-run):**

- Compare the new arm against the prior baseline arm directly.
- Surface the diff in verdict and findings; do not collapse to a
  single "improved / regressed" label.

### 6. Render the comparison report

Combine arms into a single Markdown report with a row per arm and
the structured output side-by-side:

```bash
cat > "$OUT/report.md" <<EOF
# Compare-prompts report

- Axis: harness
- Prompt: $PROMPT
- Profile: smart
- Arms: claude, codex, gemini

## Per-arm output

### claude
$(cat "$OUT/claude.md")

### codex
$(cat "$OUT/codex.md")

### gemini
$(cat "$OUT/gemini.md")

## Aggregation

<verdict table + policy outcome>
EOF
```

If the comparison is associated with a bead (e.g., a quorum review
of the bead's diff), attach the report as evidence:

```bash
ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body "$OUT/report.md"
```

## Replacing `--quorum`

The retired `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` flag
maps to this workflow:

| Old | New |
|---|---|
| `--harnesses=claude,codex,gemini` | three parallel `ddx agent run --harness <name>` calls |
| `--quorum=majority` | aggregation step 5, "majority" policy |
| `--quorum=unanimous` | aggregation step 5, "unanimous" policy |
| inline aggregated stdout | `report.md` rendered by step 6 |

The skill formalizes what `--quorum` did inline. Behavioral parity
requires choosing the same harness set, the same policy, and routing
each arm through `--profile smart` (or whatever profile the original
quorum invocation pinned).

## Replacing `agent benchmark`

The retired `ddx agent benchmark` mapped a prompt across a fixed
matrix and rendered a results table. The skill subsumes it by:

- Authoring the matrix as the per-arm dispatch list in step 3.
- Reusing the output contract in step 2 to keep arms aggregateable.
- Rendering the results table in step 6.

For benchmarking across many prompts (a prompt suite), see the
`benchmark-suite` skill — it composes this skill across a prompt
matrix.

## Anti-patterns

- **Mixing axes silently.** Varying both harness and prompt at once
  hides which variable produced the difference. Pick one axis, or
  enumerate the full matrix.
- **No output contract.** Free-form arm output is not
  aggregateable. Always include the structured output contract in
  the prompt.
- **Dropping failed arms.** A failed arm is data. Mark it as failed
  in the report — do not silently aggregate over the survivors.
- **Picking a winner mechanically.** Quorum aggregation is
  mechanical (policy → verdict). Benchmark aggregation requires
  human judgment on quality, not a counting rule.
- **One-arm "comparison".** A single dispatch is `ddx agent run`,
  not a comparison. This skill requires N ≥ 2.
- **Reusing `arm1.md` / `arm2.md` filenames.** Name files for the
  varied axis value so the report is self-describing.

## CLI reference

```bash
# Three-harness quorum (majority policy)
ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt prompt.md > out/claude.md &
ddx agent run --harness codex  --profile smart --prompt prompt.md > out/codex.md  &
ddx agent run --harness gemini --profile smart --prompt prompt.md > out/gemini.md &
wait

# Two-prompt benchmark (one harness, varying prompt)
ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt prompt-v1.md > out/v1.md &
ddx agent run --harness claude --profile smart --prompt prompt-v2.md > out/v2.md &
wait

# Two-persona comparison
ddx agent run --harness claude --persona reviewer  --prompt prompt.md > out/reviewer.md  &
ddx agent run --harness claude --persona architect --prompt prompt.md > out/architect.md &
wait

# Attach the comparison report as bead evidence
ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body out/report.md
```

## Related skills

- `adversarial-review` — N-arm dispatch with adversarial critic
  framing layered on top.
- `benchmark-suite` — composes `compare-prompts` across a prompt
  matrix for suite-level evaluation.
- `replay-bead` — re-run a prior bead's prompt under altered
  conditions; uses the replay aggregation shape from step 5.

input.command:
git commit -m "feat(skills): ship compare-prompts skill [ddx-6e0d3e80]

Adds skills/ddx/compare-prompts/ — a workflow skill that composes
ddx agent run for N-arm dispatch + result aggregation. Replaces
the retired --quorum flag and ddx agent benchmark CLI per
docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md."

│ 🥊 lefthook v2.1.6  hook: pre-commit │
╰──────────────────────────────────────╯
│  Skipping hook sync: core.hooksPath is set locally to '/Users/erik/Projects/ddx/.git/hooks'            
│
